We now have all the tools needed to draw a qualitatively accurate graph of
any differentiable function without plotting individual points. The zeros of

children.html: fff

children.value: anchor the curve to the axis. The sign of

children.html: f′f'f′

children.value:
marks the intervals of
increase and decrease identified in chapter 5, and its zeros locate the
critical points classified in section 8.1. The sign of

children.html: f′′f''f′′

children.value:
marks the convex
and concave intervals from section 7.3, and its zeros locate the inflection
points of section 7.4. Combining all this information into a single coherent
sketch is the technique of

, and it brings together nearly
everything we have developed since chapter 5.

What is the curve sketching procedure?

children.label: What is the curve sketching procedure?

children.value:
Every curve sketch follows the same sequence of steps, each building on the
last:
Determine the domain and note any restrictions, asymptotes, or symmetries.

second derivative test from section 8.1.

children.value: How do we sketch the speed profile of a test sled?

children.label: How do we sketch the speed profile of a test sled?

children.value:
A motorised sled on a straight test track performs a controlled braking
sequence. Starting from rest, the sled accelerates to a test speed, then
brakes back to a complete stop before re-accelerating. The speed of the sled,
in m/s, as a function of time

children.html: ttt
v(t)=t(t−3)2,t∈[0,4].v(t) = t(t - 3)^2, \quad t \in [0, 4].v(t)=t(t−3)2,t∈[0,4].
vvv

The domain is the closed interval

children.html: [0,4][0, 4][0,4]

children.value:
.
The function is a polynomial of degree three, continuous and differentiable
everywhere on this interval.

children.value: is negative, so the curve is concave (bends downward). For

children.value: the second derivative is positive, so the curve is convex (bends upward).

The curve starts at the origin, rises with downward curvature to the local
maximum at

children.html: (1, 4)(1,\, 4)(1,4)

children.value: , continues descending through the inflection at

children.html: (2, 2)(2,\, 2)(2,2)

children.value:
where the curvature reverses, then arcs upward to the double zero
at

children.html: (3, 0)(3,\, 0)(3,0)

children.value:
where it just touches the axis before rising again to the
endpoint at

children.html: (4, 4)(4,\, 4)(4,4)

to a stop and then re-accelerating without reversing direction.

SVELTE APP: "Curve sketching explorer"

  THREE stacked panels sharing the same t-axis [0, 4].

  TOP PANEL: v(t) = t(t-3)²
    The speed profile in dark blue.
    Filled dots in orange at the six key points from the table.
    Shaded region: light red for t ∈ [0, 2] (concave), light blue for
    t ∈ [2, 4] (convex). A dashed vertical line at t = 2 (inflection).

  MIDDLE PANEL: v'(t) = 3(t-1)(t-3)
    The first-derivative curve in red.
    Filled dots where v'(t) = 0 at t = 1 and t = 3.
    A dashed horizontal line at y = 0.
    The sign of v' labelled "+" and "−" in each subinterval.

  BOTTOM PANEL: v''(t) = 6t − 12
    A straight line in orange.
    A filled dot at the zero t = 2.
    The zero crossing labelled "inflection point of v".

  A movable cursor linked across all three panels. Dragging it shows the
  student how the value of v' in the middle panel equals the slope of v
  in the top panel, and how v'' equals the slope of v' in the middle panel.

  Purpose: the three-panel layout makes the interplay between f, f', and f''
  concrete and visual. The cursor is the key: students see all three values
  update simultaneously as they slide through the domain.

Where does curve sketching appear in engineering?

children.label: Where does curve sketching appear in engineering?

children.value:
The ability to read qualitative behaviour directly from derivatives is
valuable whenever a function is known in formula form and the engineer needs
to understand its structure quickly, without numerical simulation.

In control engineering, the step response of a system is a function of time
whose zeros (crossing times), local extrema (overshoot values), and
inflection points (moment of maximum rate of rise) each have direct
engineering significance. An engineer sketches the response curve from the
transfer function in order to check whether the system will produce
acceptable behaviour before committing to a detailed simulation. In
thermodynamics, the efficiency of a cycle as a function of one operating
parameter can be sketched to identify the optimal operating region and the
sensitivity of the efficiency to changes away from the optimum.

We have assembled the full curve sketching procedure into a seven-step
sequence and applied it to the speed profile

children.html: v(t)=t(t−3)2v(t) = t(t - 3)^2v(t)=t(t−3)2

children.value:
. The
sketch required no numerical plotting: the zeros, critical points, inflection
point, and curvature sign were all determined from

children.html: v′v'v′
v′′v''v′′
vvv
v′v'v′
v′′v''v′′

children.value:
showed how each feature of the
curve is encoded in the sign or zero of its derivatives.

Curve sketching describes a function that is already given to us. In
engineering the more common task is the reverse: we have a design goal and
must find the variable values that achieve the best outcome. Setting up and
solving such problems is the subject of the next section.
